speaker: interviewer
text: December 18, 1996. Interview with Aron Bell. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall exactly the last question that you asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were assembling in a new place in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the way we lived up until the end of the war. You got hurt in one place, you running to another-- to another place where there are woods. But the woods were our safe haven. This was the-- This was the place to survive. If there would be no woods, we would not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that 10 miles-- 10 kilometers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was a very different distance-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: --than over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Than over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason, apparently, is the people there, we were not as civilized, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe, as the Americans are. What I mean by that is, we were not mechanized. We had no cars, we had no transportation. Our transportation at best was horseback riding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in order to understand what a distance is 10 miles away over there or 10 kilometers, it's like here, 200 miles away. This is absolutely-- so maybe I don't explain myself correctly, but this is to the best of my ability that I could tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then already in '43, we had-- we had already hundreds, hundreds of people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, you know, it was an organized group of people, if you will. Tuvia was mainly the leader, took care of the people that would not fight-- women, children, older men. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Asael was the leader of the-- Asael and Zisl were the leader of the fighting group. There was a fighting group already. It had about 4 or 500 young men, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which they were as good as any and maybe better. See, a person has to learn. You have to show a person, and he learns. I'll give you an example. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One of the young men that came out from [? Nevalduk ?] to us, he was given a horse-- a young, strong, healthy young man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not going to call him by the name because people that know us and they will hear about the interview, they-- they wouldn't-- it's not right. I will not mention his name. He was given a horse. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The horse, in order to horseback riding. This was like practically as good as a rifle. If you had a horse, a good horse, and a rifle, if you're armed, you had 80% chance of surviving life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was given a horse. He tried-- now, picture for a-- for a moment. He tried-- you get on the horse in order to horseback riding, you get in from the left-hand side. So he tried to get on the horse, which he got on it, with his right foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you picture for a moment? He sits on the horse with his right foot. The young man there understood what I said. I hope you under-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the different [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from a city person to a farmer in those days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm giving an example why-- what was about my brothers. Of course, there were tough people in the city, but they were different. They grew up under different circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This very same man, I'm giving you-- not that I heard, I saw it with my own eyes. I started to laugh at him. So he gets off the horse and runs after me, wanted to hit me. Why did-- I laughed at him all my life. Later on, he turned out to be among the best. He was even among the leaders, not only a good partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then why won't you tell us his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you his name. I wouldn't tell you. I wouldn't tell you. Only a few people know about that, that specific incident that I just described to you, but it's not fair to tell his name because he's got children, he's got a surviving wife, and he was a fine individual himself, a very bright and a very good partisan. Don't forget, people, intelligent people already-- my brothers got help when they had hundreds of people. There was a man like Malbin. There was a man like Wolkowicki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a man like Frydberg Pesach. Those are all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the duties of these various men were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the leaders, what I just mentioned. They were the leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any specific duties each?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Each one had a duty. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know-- would you remember who was doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For an example, the gentleman, Wolkowicki, he was an attorney, and he worked for the NKVD before the war started, so he took take care of the internal affairs. When you get together a few hundred Jews, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have psychological problem. The results, so jealousy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that show itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were reporting to the Russian high command. Later on, there was a Russian high command where the-- all partisans was under the Russian leadership, and you had to answer to them. So there were Jews that did not like my brother. They were like NKVDists that worked for the NKVD. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So for an example, so this one, Wolkowicki, he was the man in charge that the whole group of people that you see that's-- how shall I explain to you? That was like a big business, if you will, you're running. Right? So Wolkowicki was taking care of the internal affairs. How was he taking care of them? What was he actually doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Making reports. Making reports to the Russian high command. Reports got to the Russian high command that my brothers are against communism, out of jealousy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent those reports?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two brothers, two Jewish fellas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] two Jewish men. I don't remember their names. It's really not even-- what's so important, their names. Just to look for animosity if they have children left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in charge of the internal affairs that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would he do to counteract this kind of a report? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If somebody sent a report like that, what he would he do to--

speaker: interviewee
text: He would counteract it. It isn't true, whatever had to be done. I couldn't tell you that, but it was all done in writing. It was like-- it was a legitimate-- how shall I tell you-- a legitimate piece of a military run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you officially incorporated into the Russian partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct, otherwise, would be no problem. This is-- this--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get back to details of that. What about the duties of some of the other men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other men was-- were involved to be responsible there should be enough food. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you the names, really. I couldn't tell you. You're going back 50 years. How on earth can I remember that, those names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any specific duties connected with any specific men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, I-- I know the main people. Wolkowicki was in charge. I would-- In English, I think, as I said on the internal affairs that everything is run properly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then was a fellow, Malbin, which he was-- and you use it in English. I'll tell you in Russian. He was a [RUSSIAN] he was super intelligent man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those were all-- in these parts, in these things, my brothers already needed help, which they had it, otherwise, they could never run 1,200 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned somebody called Frydberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Frydberg came also in the beginning, in summer '92. He was also part of the leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: His main job was fighting, Pesach Frydberg, yes. Mainly collaborators, though. He wanted and he was good at that, to go to collaborators. Let's assume one farmer-- you see there were farmers, they were Gentile people at the time. They would look for Jews to turn into the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One farmer tells to the other, to his neighbor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I arrested, I caught yesterday 12 Jews run out of the ghetto. First, we raped them, the women, and then we killed them. He was bragging to his neighbor, but he didn't realize that this neighbor is our friend. This neighbor would come [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to us and tell us that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you want a specific thing about Pesach Frydberg, I will tell you. There was one fellow, his name was Bellows. His name, I remember. And he bragged to his neighbors-- it was in another village, like 20, 30 kilometers away from Stankiewicze.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he bragged that he arrested about 11, 12 Jews and turned them all over, all of them, to the Nazis. And obviously, the Nazis would butcher them on the spot. And it got to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Pesach Frydberg went, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, a couple of dozen partisans to this family, and he appeared to them as a German policeman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said that he is a German policeman, other partisan. And he bragged to him that a couple of weeks ago, he caught a dozen Jews and turned over to the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they kill the whole family. I don't know how many people they had in their-- Bellows family. Burned his house, and this was the end of it. But on the way back, running from the village, the Nazis got a hold of them and shooting them, and one of the partisans got wounded. A couple got wounded, but nobody got killed. How did they take care of the wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: The best way possible. The best way you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the best way? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had doctors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had doctors, two, I think. They had abortions there, you name it. They had everything. It was like a little town. It was a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me about the little town. What do you mean by little town? What did they have? What kind of organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little town. They-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you had to feed people, right? So they had a place where to keep the groceries, food. A man was in charge of that. There was cooks. There were people to fix the shoe. There were people to fix a rifle. There were people that they could entertain you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Entertainment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, they had entertainment, and how.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of entertainment? Singing, obvious, Russian style. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had very beautiful-- and there were dancers. There was beautiful singers.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would make a stage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would make a stage. It was like a little town. You get up in the morning, you wash your face, and each one had a responsibility. This one stands guard. This one washes. The other one fixes the shoe. The other one-- the third one fixes a rifle. It was a little town built. And the word got around to the Russian partisans that you go to the Bielskis, and you get anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to fix-- you want to fix a rifle? They'll fix it for you. You want to fix a coat or a jacket, they'll fix it for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get paid for their services?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they wouldn't get paid for their service. There's no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any exchange of favors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exchange of favors, I would assume so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you exactly exchange. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look, I was a kid. If you'll feed me, I'll apparently do something for you if you will ask me to, if I can. A human exchange, but no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russian partisans frequently come to your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They were free. We had problems with the Russian partisans too. There was-- some of them were anti-Semites, Jew haters. Some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did these problems show themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: A group of partisans from our brigade went to a certain village to get food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was another Russian brigade, which the commander was a Russian captain from the army. Tall, handsome as they come. I remember him like today, like yesterday.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Held me on in his hands. Viktor Panchenko. He's still alive. I spoke to him on the telephone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To make a story short, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our-- a group of our people went to the village, got food. And then from his brigade came the Russian partisans to the same village, and they said, we were robbed by the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: From his brigade-- about 10, 12-- took away the rifles from our group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Took away the arms. Without arms, you're nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You're nothing. Anybody can kill you, right? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Someway, somehow, it got very quickly to my-- one of my brothers, in fact, to Zisl, that he's-- from his brigade, from the Viktor Panchenko, a group of people took away, and they still in the village. They still in this and this house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He went with his own 10 men, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he carries them out well, and he took not only our-- our arms back, but he took the arms away from them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Then it got to the commander, to that Viktor Panchenko, to make this story short, that Viktor Panchenko then found my brother, Zisl, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what are you making trouble? What is this? What are you, a hero? You're taking away [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arms from my people, the Russian commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: He doesn't tell the whole story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So my brother Zisl told him, if you think that you a hero, try to take it back, and you'll find out what kind of a hero I am. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm translating you word by word. You wanted to know what kind of a brothers I had. When my brother was talking to him, he walked around with a short machine gun. It had 71 bullets. You can spray that within a second, and you can make it that each bullet will come out one by one. I don't know if you know anything about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He held his finger on the trigger against this commander. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the back of my brother was Viktor Sadutant. He was an Uzbek. To him, to kill a person was easier than for you to clean the-- to take a hair off the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Curban. Had an automatic pistol right in the back of my brother's head, which my brother did not know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By a lot came-- by pure luck, showed up, Platon, which he was the highest commander of the whole Russian partisan's estate of White Russia. He knew both. He knew my brother, and he knew the Russian commander. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that Platon went, the commander, Sadutant, had his pistol behind my brother's neck. He knocked his hand down, the chief of all the partisans, the state of White Russia. And that's why my brother find out that he had a gun behind his neck. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he straighten it out. Obvious, it should not happen again, but there were Jewish-- there-- there were Russian partisans. Did this happen inside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. This happened outside of camp. This happened in one of the villages. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This happened one-- in one of the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everything that happened then became common knowledge in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Of course. There's nothing to hide. It-- everybody knew about that. How can you hide when 15 people of our partisans, and they-- they took away all the arms from them? How can you hide that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me personally? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I would assume so. If I were not frightened, I wouldn't go hide myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, while in the camp, were you aware of-- on a day-to-day basis, were you aware of the dangers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was I aware of the dangers? I didn't think I was aware as much as the grown person is. I don't think that a kid could-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] could cope with that, if he-- if-- that a kid can understand [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what life and what death is. But I guess-- why would I-- when I saw that they beating my father, the daylights out of him, I hide myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, inside-- inside the camp. Did you ever think whether this was temporary or a permanent arrangement in your life? Did you ever think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I overheard from the elders, that the war is not for-- from older people, that the war is not forever, but to say that I was full of fear, know that those days already, of course you had fear, because you knew you-- everybody is out to get you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just because you're Jewish. I assume that everybody-- it's-- you see, here is a thing that it depends how one manages to live with fear. This should have been, apparently, the question. But if it's a normal individual, he must have fear unless he's abnormal, then he's fearless.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how you manage. You think my brother, when he walks with an open machine gun, all he has to do is-- is-- is touch the trigger of the machine gun, and then people get killed. You think he had no fear? Of course, he had fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was speaking of you as a child. To what degree--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would assume I had fear, otherwise, why would I go and run away and hide myself when I saw they beat my father up? So I knew if he has a rifle, and he's there, I have to run the other way. I assume it's out of fear, is it not?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brigade ever encounter Polish partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] No. In our area, when we came to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], that's where we heard about Polish partisans, but we really never came encounter with them. The only ones that were were the Russian partisans, and there was another Jewish small brigade. I remember in [NON-ENGLISH], he had about 150 people, women and children. He was a Jew that was born in Minsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zorin. This was his name. He is the only one that was in the-- there were little groups, like bandits. There were people like those that survived the war, like a group of 10, very few. Later on, they-- they were caught by the Russian partisans and brought to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were brought to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. To the-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke of a fighting force of 500.

speaker: interviewee
text: At least. They were 4 or 500 young men. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of fighting did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did everything later on. They lead together with the Russian partisans. They-- They would wait for a convoy with trucks, kill the Nazis as much as they could, and take their arms away and take whatever there was away from them. They burned bridges, they-- whatever they could, whatever they-- to hurt the Nazis, that's what they did. Of course, they were fighting. There were orders from the higher ups. That's why it was divided. The fighting group one, and women and children in the other one. It was orders by the-- from the higher Russian command. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they should divide the women and children, and have a fighting group, and go out and fight the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those two groups located in separate--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far one from the other. Not far. Like 5 kilometers, 10 kilometers away. Sometimes they work together for a while. But then, towards the end of-- in the middle of '43, when we went to [NON-ENGLISH], there already, we work together, the fighting and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under what circumstances did you go to the [NON-ENGLISH]? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What made you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because over here, in our area where we originated, where we were born and brought up, they attacked us more often-- more often, so we had to look for bigger areas. And when you come to a [NON-ENGLISH], this is like endless woods. There are places there which a human being was not there yet, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there already. Although I think it was in '44 that they attacked us even there. The whole Nazi army attacked us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the place differ physically from the first place you were at? You said no human being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, differently because over there, you had a lot of room. You didn't have as much fear. You did not have to be so quiet, like at first, in the smaller woods, where we were born and brought up. Here were no villages close by, and those were endless, miles and miles worth of woods that-- that a human being was not there. If you cross a little island, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] as I said, there was no-- there was miles of woods, endless--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.
